entries.text: I watched the NASDAQ climb to 5048 in March 2000. I was sitting in a coffee shop that no longer exists. A kid next to me was explaining to his friend how Pets.com would change retail forever. Six months later, gone.
In 1999 I was offered a stake in a company that did 'real-time sports scores on WAP devices.' I passed. The domain they wanted to use was taken. I still think about that sometimes.
I sold my first thing on eBay in 1998. A box of old computer magazines. Made $47. Thought I was a genius. Lost money on shipping.

domain_stories.description: Tales of domain name acquisitions and waiting

entries.text: I own three domain names from 1998. Not good ones. Not bad ones. Just... mine. I've had renewal notices every year since 2001. I pay $35/year for each. I don't know why. I just don't sell.
I almost bought 'internetyellowpages.com' in 1999 from a guy who was letting it expire. He wanted $2000. I passed. Someone else bought it. It's still up. I check sometimes.

I've heard 'this changes everything' seventeen times since 1995. The internet. P2P. Social media. Mobile. Cloud. Crypto. AI. Each one was supposed to end traditional business. None of them did. They just added more noise.

The blockchain thing. I lived through Beanie Babies. I lived through tulip bulbs. I lived through dot-com pets. I know what a speculative bubble looks like from the inside. Doesn't mean I don't understand why people get excited.

I found everything on AltaVista in 1997. It was the only search engine that worked. Then Google came along and said 'relevance is a solvable problem.' They were right. Nobody saw AltaVista's death coming.

GeoCities was the internet for people who had something to say. Cluttered, chaotic, alive. Then it got bought and turned into something that died. The internet was better when it was run by people who just wanted to share.
I have a Compaq Deskpro in my closet. Pentium 233. It still boots. I don't know why I keep it. It was cutting-edge in 1998. Now it's a conversation piece.
I have a box of ZIP disks somewhere. 100MB each. I think there's still one with a business plan on it. From 1999. The kind of plan that leads nowhere.
